01-setinterval-counter.title: Counter with setInterval
01-setinterval-counter.summary: This example shows how to use setInterval to run code periodically. It's fundamental to understand before learning about Web Workers, since counters are a common way to demonstrate how the main thread can get blocked.
01-setinterval-counter.whatToWatch: Watch the two lanes: when the counter runs in a worker, the main thread stays free and the UI responds. When it runs on the main thread, that lane is blocked and the UI freezes.
01-setinterval-counter.note: This counter works perfectly because the main thread is free. When the main thread is busy with heavy work, the same counter freezes — which is exactly why you move it to a worker. The comparison above shows that difference.

takeaways.title: Key takeaways
items: setInterval runs its callback on the main thread
If the main thread is busy, the intervals are delayed
This is the fundamental problem Web Workers solve
takeaways.tip: Use requestAnimationFrame instead of setInterval for smoother animations.

03-basic-communication.title: Basic communication
03-basic-communication.summary: The "Hello World" of Web Workers: you type a message and send it to the worker. The worker receives it, processes it, and replies back. That's how threads talk to each other — postMessage in both directions.
03-basic-communication.whatToWatch: → leaves the main thread toward the worker, ← the processed reply comes back. The ms number is the REAL round-trip: crossing the thread costs a fraction of a millisecond — almost nothing.
03-basic-communication.note: postMessage CLONES the data (structured clone): it's serialized on the way out and deserialized on the way in, so the worker gets a COPY, not the reference. The cost isn't crossing the thread — that's sub-ms — it's copying: it grows with payload size. A good practical budget: ~10KB of JSON. For large data, transferables move without copying (example 07).

takeaways.title: Key takeaways
items: postMessage() sends data from main to worker and back again
Data is automatically cloned (structured clone)
onmessage receives messages on both sides
Communication is async: the main thread doesn't block waiting for the reply
takeaways.tip: Always handle the onerror event to catch silent worker failures.

04-offloading-computation.title: Offload heavy work
04-offloading-computation.summary: Here's the payoff of Web Workers: getting heavy work off the main thread. We count prime numbers — a deliberately intensive computation — and run it on both sides so you feel the difference.
04-offloading-computation.whatToWatch: Run it in a worker: the live timer keeps ticking and you can still interact — the UI never janks. Run the SAME computation on the main thread: the whole page freezes (no repaint, no clicks) until it's done.
04-offloading-computation.note: Any work that takes more than ~16ms blocks a frame and shows. A worker runs in true parallel on another thread: the computation takes the same time, but the main thread stays free to respond. The result comes back via postMessage (cloned).

takeaways.title: Key takeaways
items: Heavy work on the main thread freezes the whole UI while it runs
A worker runs in real parallel: the main thread stays responsive
The computation isn't faster in the worker — but the UI doesn't jank
The result returns via postMessage (structured clone); for large data, transferables (example 07)
takeaways.tip: Rule of thumb: if a task can take longer than one frame (~16ms), send it to a worker.

05-error-handling.title: Error handling
05-error-handling.summary: An error inside a worker doesn't crash your page. The task here parses JSON: a valid payload returns its key count; a broken one makes JSON.parse throw a real SyntaxError. The worker lets that error propagate and the main thread catches it.
05-error-handling.whatToWatch: Send a valid JSON (✓ the result is logged) and then a broken one (✗ the main thread catches the error via onerror). Notice the app stays responsive: you can keep running tasks after the error — the worker didn't die.
05-error-handling.note: An uncaught error inside the worker fires the onerror event on the main thread's Worker object; it does NOT crash the page or kill the worker. For more control, catch the error INSIDE the worker (try/catch) and report it as a normal postMessage: that way you can type the error and add context.

takeaways.title: Key takeaways
items: An uncaught error in the worker reaches the main thread as worker.onerror
The page doesn't crash: the worker stays alive and you can run more tasks
ErrorEvent carries message, filename and lineno to diagnose
For more control, use try/catch inside the worker and report the error via postMessage
takeaways.tip: Call event.preventDefault() in onerror to silence the red console log if you already surface the error in your UI.

06-lifecycle-termination.title: Lifecycle and termination
06-lifecycle-termination.summary: A worker doesn't live forever: you create it, it runs, and you terminate it. Here you start a long task that advances step by step and can cut it off midway. terminate() kills the worker instantly — the in-flight step is lost and that worker can't be reused.
06-lifecycle-termination.whatToWatch: Start the task and watch the bar climb. Hit Terminate midway: progress freezes where it was and the worker stops existing. To run again you must create a new one (the Start button does that). If you let it finish, the worker closes itself with self.close().
06-lifecycle-termination.note: terminate() is abrupt: no cleanup, no goodbye — the worker dies with its in-flight work (the pending setTimeout goes with it). A terminated worker can't be restarted: you make a new one. From the inside, a worker can close itself with self.close() once its work is done.

takeaways.title: Key takeaways
items: worker.terminate() kills the worker instantly from the main thread
In-flight work is discarded: no cleanup, no close event
A terminated worker can't be reused: create a new one to run again
self.close() lets the worker close itself when it's done
takeaways.tip: Terminate workers you no longer use: they keep holding memory and a thread even while idle.

07-transferable-objects.title: Transferable objects
07-transferable-objects.summary: postMessage normally CLONES the data (structured clone). For large data — an ArrayBuffer — that costs. A Transferable is passed without copying (zero-copy): the buffer changes owner to the worker. The catch: the sender's buffer is left detached, empty and unusable.
07-transferable-objects.whatToWatch: Send the same buffer both ways and compare the round-trip. Transferring is near-instant even for a big buffer; cloning takes longer because it copies byte by byte. And watch the side effect: after transferring, the main's buffer is DETACHED (0 bytes); when cloning, the main keeps its copy.
07-transferable-objects.note: Transferring doesn't copy: it moves ownership of the buffer to the worker, which is why the sender's side is left detached (byteLength 0) and using it throws. Transferable types: ArrayBuffer, MessagePort, ImageBitmap, OffscreenCanvas, and ReadableStream/WritableStream. If you need the data on both sides, clone; if not, transfer and save the copy.

takeaways.title: Key takeaways
items: postMessage clones by default; the 2nd argument (transfer list) transfers without copying
Transferring is zero-copy: instant even for large buffers
A transferred buffer is left detached on the sender's side (byteLength 0)
Transferables: ArrayBuffer, MessagePort, ImageBitmap, OffscreenCanvas, streams
takeaways.tip: If you won't touch the buffer again after sending it, transfer it: same data, without the cost of the copy.

08-shared-worker.title: SharedWorker
08-shared-worker.summary: A regular Worker belongs to whoever created it. A SharedWorker is ONE, shared by several connections — several tabs, or here, several panels. They all talk to the same instance and see the same state: the counter doesn't live in each panel, it lives in the worker.
08-shared-worker.whatToWatch: The same number in both panels, and the same worker id up top. Add in one and watch it jump in the other: they're not two synced copies, it's ONE variable inside the worker. Add a third connection and you'll see it inherit the current value.
08-shared-worker.note: Each connection arrives via onconnect and brings its own MessagePort; you must call port.start() to receive. State lives in the worker, not the pages, which is why a new connection inherits the counter. Unlike example 03 (main↔worker, 1 to 1), here it's N to 1. Inspect it at chrome://inspect/#workers. If the browser doesn't support SharedWorker, this demo falls back to a simulated backend (you'll see it labeled).

takeaways.title: Key takeaways
items: A SharedWorker is ONE instance shared by several connections (N to 1)
You connect via onconnect and one MessagePort per client (remember port.start())
State lives in the worker, not each page: a new connection inherits it
Useful to coordinate same-origin tabs without a server in between
takeaways.tip: A SharedWorker stays alive as long as at least one connection is open; it closes when the last one leaves.

09-worker-limits.title: Limits of parallelism
09-worker-limits.summary: Workers run truly in parallel, but only as far as your CPU goes. navigator.hardwareConcurrency tells you how many threads can run at once. Past that number, workers don't run "more in parallel": they share the same cores. Here we run K copies of the same computation at once and measure how long they take.
09-worker-limits.whatToWatch: Watch the times column. While K stays under your cores, the time stays about the same (they all fit in parallel). When K exceeds them, the time climbs: there's nowhere left to run them, so they take turns. The threshold is exactly your hardwareConcurrency.
09-worker-limits.note: navigator.hardwareConcurrency is a hint, not a guarantee: the OS shares cores across everything running. Spawning extra workers doesn't speed up the computation and does cost memory and startup overhead. Rule of thumb: for CPU work, no more workers than cores. That's what a pool is for (example 10).

takeaways.title: Key takeaways
items: navigator.hardwareConcurrency = how many threads actually run at once
More workers than cores isn't faster: they share the same cores
The time stays flat up to the core count, then climbs
Each extra worker costs memory and startup, with no CPU gain
takeaways.tip: For CPU work, size your pool to navigator.hardwareConcurrency; beyond that only adds overhead.

10-worker-pool.title: Worker pool
10-worker-pool.summary: The fix for the previous example's limit: instead of creating one worker per task, you keep a fixed pool of N workers and reuse them to drain a queue of many tasks. Each worker finishes a task and grabs the next. Here: 4 workers process 24 tasks.
10-worker-pool.whatToWatch: Watch the queue drain: tasks go from pending to running (picked up by a worker) to done. There are only 4 slots and they're reused — check each one's counter: it has already dispatched several. Below, the number that matters: 4 workers were created, not 24.
10-worker-pool.note: The pool is a scheduler you write on the main thread; the worker has no idea (it just computes). Reusing workers avoids the cost of creating and destroying one per task, and keeps concurrency capped at N (without exceeding the cores, as example 09 warned). Size N to the work: for CPU, near navigator.hardwareConcurrency.

takeaways.title: Key takeaways
items: A pool reuses N workers for M tasks, instead of one per task
Each worker finishes its task and takes the next from the queue
N workers are created (here 4), not M (24): less memory and startup
The pool is a main-thread scheduler; the worker only does the compute
takeaways.tip: Size the pool to the work: for CPU, near the core count; for I/O you can have more, since they barely use CPU.

11-backpressure-scheduling.title: Backpressure
11-backpressure-scheduling.summary: postMessage doesn't block or warn if the worker can't keep up: if the producer sends faster than the consumer processes, the worker's internal queue grows without bound (memory and latency). Backpressure is flow control: you only send while you have credit and wait for the ack before continuing.
11-backpressure-scheduling.whatToWatch: Run both sides with the same 40 tasks. Without backpressure: you fire everything at once, the in-flight peak jumps to 40 and the last reply comes back very late (it waited behind all 40). With backpressure: you wait for the ack (window of 3), the peak never goes past 3 and the last reply's latency stays bounded — the producer moves at the consumer's pace.
11-backpressure-scheduling.note: Latency is the consequence measured here: without flow control, everything you overproduce sits queued eating memory and each reply waits behind the whole line — so the LAST one arrives very late. A credit window (send N, wait for ack, send the next) caps the queue at N and keeps that latency low. It's the same principle as TCP and streams.

takeaways.title: Key takeaways
items: postMessage does no backpressure: it queues everything you throw at it
Producer faster than consumer → the queue grows without bound
A credit window (send, wait for ack, send) caps the queue
A bounded queue = bounded memory and low latency
takeaways.tip: If the source supports it, use a ReadableStream: it brings backpressure built in, no manual wiring.

12-shared-array-buffer.title: SharedArrayBuffer
12-shared-array-buffer.summary: So far everything traveled as messages: postMessage clones (03) or transfers (07). A SharedArrayBuffer is different: main and worker share the SAME memory, not a copy. The worker writes a number there with Atomics and the main reads it directly — without a single postMessage in between.
12-shared-array-buffer.whatToWatch: Start it and watch the number climb. The worker increments it in the shared memory; the main reads that same memory with Atomics.load. The messages-exchanged counter stays at 0: there's no back-and-forth, it's literally the same variable in both threads.
12-shared-array-buffer.note: Atomics guarantees coherent reads and writes when several threads touch the same memory (without it you'd get race conditions). SharedArrayBuffer is powerful but only exists if the page is cross-origin isolated: you must serve with the COOP and COEP headers. Without them, typeof SharedArrayBuffer is 'undefined' (here you'll see a labeled simulated backend).

takeaways.title: Key takeaways
items: SharedArrayBuffer shares the SAME memory between threads, not a copy
The worker writes and the main reads the same cell, without postMessage
Atomics prevents race conditions when touching shared memory
Only available with cross-origin isolation (COOP/COEP headers)
takeaways.tip: Use Atomics.wait/notify to synchronize threads over shared memory without polling.

13-graceful-degradation.title: Graceful degradation
13-graceful-degradation.summary: Not every environment has Web Workers (old runtimes, some SSR contexts). Graceful degradation is: you detect the feature (typeof Worker) and, if it's missing, run the SAME work on the main thread. The app works everywhere — better where workers exist, just as correct where they don't.
13-graceful-degradation.whatToWatch: Hit Process: if Worker exists, it runs off-thread and the UI stays smooth. Tick "simulate browser without Worker" and process again: it runs the same function on the main thread —the UI freezes— but the result is exactly the same. Same code, two paths chosen by feature detection.
13-graceful-degradation.note: The key is sharing the logic: a pure function (here, counting primes) that the worker and the main thread can both run. The worker is just the wrapper that runs it on another thread. So the fallback isn't duplicated code: it's the same function, minus the worker. Detect once and pick the path; never assume Worker exists.

takeaways.title: Key takeaways
items: Detect the feature (typeof Worker) instead of assuming it exists
Without Worker, run the SAME work on the main: you degrade the UX, not the function
Share the pure logic between worker and fallback (don't duplicate it)
The app works everywhere: better with workers, correct without them
takeaways.tip: The same applies to OffscreenCanvas, WebGL, etc.: detect and degrade, don't assume support.

14-offscreen-canvas.title: Rendering off the main thread (OffscreenCanvas)
14-offscreen-canvas.summary: A <canvas> can hand its control to a worker with transferControlToOffscreen(). From then on, the worker draws and animates on its own —it has its own render loop— without going through the main thread. The result? The animation stays perfectly smooth even when the main thread is blocked by heavy JavaScript. It's the platform's first example about PIXELS, not numbers: two twin clocks, and only one survives when you freeze the main thread.
14-offscreen-canvas.whatToWatch: Start both clocks: they tick in sync. Now hit «Block main 2.5s». The left clock (worker) keeps sweeping without skipping a frame; the right one (main) freezes like a photo. Look at the time printed inside each canvas: the worker's advanced smoothly; the main's jumped all at once when it unblocked, because those frames were never painted.
14-offscreen-canvas.note: transferControlToOffscreen() is one-way: the <canvas> becomes «detached» on the main thread and only the worker can draw it. The worker has no DOM and no document —it couldn't touch the page if it wanted to— but it does have requestAnimationFrame and its 2D context over the OffscreenCanvas. That's why, while the main thread is blocked on JS, its render loop doesn't stall: it runs on its own event loop, not the main thread's.

takeaways.title: What you take away
items: transferControlToOffscreen() transfers control of the <canvas> to the worker; from then on the main thread can't draw it
The worker runs its own render loop (requestAnimationFrame lives inside the worker too): it animates without depending on the main thread
If the main thread blocks on synchronous JS, the worker's animation doesn't notice; only what the main draws freezes
The worker has no DOM access: it gets an OffscreenCanvas and its drawing context, nothing else
takeaways.tip: Reserve OffscreenCanvas for rendering that must stay smooth under load: data viz, games, live charts. Pass colors/tokens in by message: there's no CSS inside.
